brand.name: PhD Match DE
brand.tagline: Doctoral research in German life sciences — curated.
brand.subtitle: Cancer · Immunology · Neuroscience

home.hero_title: Find your German life-sciences PhD — without disappearing into the noise.
home.hero_lead: The leading research centres for cancer, immunology and neuroscience in one place. AI-assisted matching of your CV to specific labs — and ready-to-send outreach emails to principal investigators.

match.title: CV Match
match.subtitle: Paste your CV and let AI rank labs by fit.
match.hero_title: Match your CV against every PhD programme.
match.hero_lead: Paste your CV — Claude analyses your methods, master's topic, and interests, then ranks every programme by fit. Each match comes with reasoning, strengths and gaps, and concrete advice on what to emphasise in your application.

step1.label: Step 01
step1.title: Paste CV
step1.intro: Copy your CV or motivation letter into the box. The more detail (methods, thesis topic, software skills, languages), the more precise the ranking.
step1.placeholder:
Example:

Gian H.
MSc Humanbiologie · University of Greifswald · 2025
Master thesis: Role of microglia in…
Methods: Western blot, qPCR, flow cytometry, immunohistochemistry, scRNA-seq analysis in R
Languages: Arabic (native), German (B2), English (C1)
Internships: …
Publications / posters: …

Research interests: tumour immunology, neuroinflammation
step1.min_chars: At least 200 characters needed for meaningful analysis

step3.label: Step 03
step3.title: Results
step3.intro: Programmes ranked by fit. Click any programme to see full reasoning.
step3.match_tips: What to emphasise in your application
step3.no_results: No analysis yet. Start with Step 01.

errors.no_key: Claude API key missing. Add VITE_ANTHROPIC_KEY to .env.
errors.too_short: CV too short — please paste at least 200 characters.

outreach.title: Outreach
outreach.subtitle: Initiative email to professors — the most important channel in Germany.
outreach.hero_title: Open the door before the position is even advertised.
outreach.hero_lead: Roughly 70% of German PhD positions are filled via direct personal contact. Use this to draft a short, precise email — in two minutes — to the professors whose work fits your profile.

step1.label: Step 01
step1.title: Recipient & programme
step1.lab_focus_placeholder: e.g. tumour immunology, T cell exhaustion in solid tumours

step2.label: Step 02
step2.title: Strategy & language

inquiry.name: Inquiry
inquiry.desc: Brief and polite: 'Are you accepting doctoral students?' The standard first contact.

paper.name: Specific paper
paper.desc: Reference a specific publication. Strongest impact — but demands homework.

methods.name: Methods bridge
methods.desc: Links your methods to the lab's. Best when you have a clear technical profile.

step2.paper_reference_placeholder: e.g. 'Your 2024 Cell paper on T cell exhaustion in melanoma especially caught my attention'
step2.no_profile_yet: No profile yet. Run a CV-Match or paste a CV below.

tips.title: Tips for effective outreach
items: Keep it short: 120–180 words, max three paragraphs.
Personalise the subject line — never 'PhD application'.
Mention a concrete research detail, not just the topic area.
Ask a specific question at the end — it invites a reply.
Attach a PDF version of your CV (in the actual email, not here).

errors.no_key: Claude API key missing. Add VITE_ANTHROPIC_KEY to .env.
errors.no_profile: Load a profile or paste a CV.
errors.no_paper: Name the paper briefly (strategy: Specific paper).

labs.title: Labs & PIs
labs.subtitle: Specific professors — the people you'll actually write to.
labs.hero_title: The people behind the programmes — curated by person, not just institution.
labs.hero_lead: Submitting to a programme is one thing — but in Germany, every successful PhD ends up at a specific person's bench. Here are 45 named research-group leaders with their current focus, lab URL, and direct contact path.

profile.title: Profile
profile.subtitle: Personal data and CV material.
profile.hero_title: Your data in one place — the whole toolkit runs from here.
profile.hero_lead: Save your CV, methods, and languages once. CV-Match, Cover Letter, and Outreach pick it up automatically. Works locally without an account — or syncs via Supabase when connected.

e.g.

cv.title: CV & skills

account.title: Account
account.no_supabase: Supabase is not configured. The profile is stored locally (in your browser) only. To use across devices, set VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY in .env.
account.sync_help: Local changes are written to your cloud profile. Other devices can pull the same data via 'Pull from cloud'.

danger.title: Clear data
danger.body: Removes all stored profile data from this browser. Your Tracker, saved cover letters, and outreach log are kept separately.

library.title: Library
library.subtitle: Curated literature, textbooks, courses, funding, and databases.
library.hero_title: The most important things to read, learn, and apply to — pre-selected.
library.hero_lead: A carefully chosen collection of landmark papers, textbooks, online courses, scholarships, conferences, and databases. Every entry is real, verified, and curated for PhDs in cancer, immunology, and neuroscience.

starter_path.title: 5-week reading path
starter_path.intro: A structured entry into your field. One paper per week, with notes on why this order makes sense.

compass.title: Compass
compass.subtitle: How a PhD in Germany actually works — pathways, methods, research literacy.
compass.hero_title: Understand the road to a doctorate — before you get lost looking.
compass.hero_lead: A structured guide to the German life-sciences PhD: from master's graduation to viva. Research tools, literature strategies, and an AI assistant trained on your specific field.

basics.title: How does a German PhD actually work?
basics.intro: There is no single "German PhD". There are two very different models — and the choice shapes funding, structure, and success.

structured.label: Structured programme
structured.title: Graduate School / Doctoral programme
structured.description: Organised programme with annual calls, mandatory seminars, a thesis committee of 2–3 professors, clear milestones, and a cohort of fellow PhD students. Ideal for international applicants.
structured.examples: HIGS DKFZ · IMPRS · HBIGS Heidelberg · EIPP EMBL

individual.label: Individual PhD
individual.title: Classical doctoral path
individual.description: Direct employment in a professor's lab. No central application process — you write initiative cover letters. Over 60% of German PhDs follow this path.
pros: Possible without English as main language
individual.examples: Uni Bonn · Uni Heidelberg · Charité · Goethe · TU Dortmund · Uni Bielefeld

items.title: Preparation & application
items.what: Clarify research interests, identify labs, write outreach or structured application, interview (often 1–3 hours).

items.title: Onboarding & topic-finding
items.what: Learn methods, pilot experiments, deep reading, finalise topic with supervisor. Draft research proposal.

items.title: Main research phase
items.what: Experiments, data collection, regular lab meetings, conference contributions, first manuscripts. This is where publications emerge.

items.title: Publications & writing
items.what: Submit first-author papers, write thesis (cumulative or monograph). Typically 3 publications for a cumulative thesis.

items.title: Submission & viva
items.what: Submit dissertation, await reviews (typ. 2–3 months), oral defence (Disputation or Rigorosum, 60–90 min).

basics.tip_body: In Germany, around 70% of PhD positions are filled via direct personal contact with professors — before they are even advertised. The `Outreach` module of this tool helps you open exactly that door.

research.title: Research & literature — the toolkit
research.intro: A supervisor does not expect you to be an expert in advance — but they do expect you to know how to research. These tools are essential equipment.

tools.pubmed: Biomedical literature — curated by the US National Library of Medicine. First stop for cancer/immuno/neuro.
tools.scholar: Broader coverage than PubMed, including preprints and grey literature. Citation tracking.
tools.europe_pmc: European counterpart of PubMed with full-text access and ORCID integration.
tools.biorxiv: Biology preprints — newest, not-yet peer-reviewed research. Essential for current trends.
tools.medrxiv: Preprints in medicine and clinical research.
tools.zotero: Free open-source reference manager. Browser connector, BibTeX export, Word/LibreOffice integration.
tools.mendeley: Elsevier reference manager with PDF annotation and social features.
tools.connected_papers: Visualises a paper and its scientific neighbourhood as a graph — perfect for exploring a new field.
tools.research_rabbit: AI-powered literature explorer. Finds similar papers, authors, new publications.
tools.scite: Shows whether papers cite supporting, neutral or contrasting — quality control.
tools.overleaf: Online LaTeX editor for the dissertation. Real-time collaboration with supervisors.
tools.obsidian: Knowledge management with Markdown and backlinks — many PhDs use it as a second brain.
tools.geneontology: GO — functional annotation of genes and gene products. Essential for molecular biology.
tools.uniprot: Protein database with sequences, structures, and functions.
tools.tcga: The Cancer Genome Atlas — largest public cancer dataset.

workflow.title: Recommended workflow
steps: Start with a review article on your topic (PubMed, filter "Review", last 5 years).
Drop the paper into Connected Papers — you immediately see the research cluster.
Save key papers in Zotero, with tags per sub-topic.
Weekly alert in Google Scholar / Europe PMC for your search terms.
Follow leading labs on bioRxiv in your field.
Notes per paper: question, method, result, critique — in Obsidian or a markdown vault.

fields.title: Research fields — where to look
fields.intro: Cancer, immunology and neuroscience are huge. These overviews help you pick a sub-topic and find the right labs.

cancer.title: Cancer research
cancer.search_terms: tumor microenvironment · CAR-T · cancer immunology · oncogene · MYC · p53 · liquid biopsy · single-cell tumor

immunology.title: Immunology
immunology.search_terms: T cell exhaustion · innate immunity · inflammasome · autoimmunity · single-cell immunology · NK cells · regulatory T cells

neuroscience.title: Neuroscience
neuroscience.search_terms: alpha-synuclein · tau · neuroinflammation · synaptic plasticity · connectome · optogenetics · iPSC neurons · microglia

writing.title: Writing — proposal, motivation letter, dissertation
writing.intro: Academic writing in Germany follows clear conventions. These building blocks save weeks of learning curve.

expose.title: The Exposé / Research Proposal
expose.what: A 3–8 page document often required with applications (especially for individual PhDs and scholarships). It shows: you understand the field, have a concrete question, and know the methods.

sections.name: Title & background
sections.purpose: 1 page. Current state of knowledge with 5–10 key references.

sections.name: Research question & hypothesis
sections.purpose: 0.5–1 page. 1–3 specific questions with theoretically grounded hypothesis.

sections.name: Methods
sections.purpose: 2–3 pages. Methods design, statistical power, pilot data if available.

sections.name: Expected results & relevance
sections.purpose: 0.5–1 page. What do we learn that's new? Clinical relevance?

motivation.title: Motivation letter (application)
structure: Who you are (academic background + master's topic in 3 sentences).
Why this programme/this lab (specific! PI name, specific publications, methods).
What you can contribute (methods experience, languages, soft skills).

dissertation.title: Dissertation: monograph vs cumulative
dissertation.mono: Classical coherent work (150–250 pages). Common in humanities, rarer in life sciences.
dissertation.cumulative: Collection of 2–4 peer-reviewed first-author papers + framing text (40–80 pages of introduction and discussion). Standard in life sciences.

writing.tip: Important: language, style and structure depend strongly on the programme and supervisor. Always ask for successful example proposals from past PhDs.

timeline.title: 36-month timeline — realistic
timeline.intro: An ideal structured life-sciences PhD. For individual PhDs, phases typically extend by 6–12 months.

assistant.title: Research assistant
assistant.intro: Ask a concrete question about your research area — from methods to literature strategy to sub-topic choice. The assistant is tuned for German life-sciences PhDs.
assistant.placeholder: Example: I'm interested in neurodegeneration, but don't know Alzheimer's and Parkinson's in detail. Where should I start?
examples: What are the 5 essential papers to read before applying for a tumour immunology position?
How do I choose between a structured and an individual PhD?
Which methods should I master before starting a neuroscience PhD?
How do I write a research proposal for a cancer genomics PhD?
assistant.no_key: Note: An Anthropic API key in .env is required for the assistant to respond.
